target.title: fix(mattermost): backfill thread history only on first-sighting recovery
target.body:
## Summary

- Backfill a Mattermost thread from the server **only on first-sighting recovery** (gateway restart / session clear), not on every empty pending-history window.
- Fixes the regression flagged in review: ordinary active-thread follow-ups no longer rehydrate the whole server thread or add an awaited REST call to the inbound path.

## Motivation

Closes #93204.

When the bot is re-mentioned inside an existing Mattermost thread after a gateway restart or session clear, the in-memory pending-history window is empty, so it replied without thread context. The first version of this fix fetched the server thread whenever that window was empty.

As [@clawsweeper correctly caught](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/93234#issuecomment), the shared turn kernel clears the pending-history window after **every** successful dispatch (`src/channels/turn/kernel.ts` → `clearPendingHistoryAfterTurn`). So an empty window is also the **normal steady state** for an active thread — backfilling on a bare empty window therefore:

1. rehydrated the full server thread on ordinary follow-up mentions, and
2. added an awaited Mattermost REST call to the inbound path on those follow-ups.

## The fix

New pure gate `shouldBackfillThreadFromServer` (in `thread-backfill.ts`) distinguishes recovery from steady state by **process lifetime**:

- It tracks thread roots already serviced this process in a caller-owned `Set`.
- The **first** empty-window sighting of a never-serviced root = genuine restart/session-clear recovery → backfill.
- Any later empty window for an already-serviced root = the kernel's normal post-turn clear → **do not** backfill.
- A root is marked serviced even when its window already had context, so a later kernel-cleared window can never re-trigger recovery.

`monitor.ts` now owns a per-process `backfilledThreadRoots` set and routes the decision through this gate. The fetch fires at most once per root per process lifetime, and never on ordinary post-turn empty windows.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** an active Mattermost thread, after the turn kernel clears its pending-history window post-turn, must NOT re-fetch and inject the full server thread on the next mention (the review's P1 regression). Only the first empty window for a never-serviced root (restart / session clear) backfills.
- **Real environment tested:** the shipped `thread-backfill.ts` functions (`shouldBackfillThreadFromServer`, `buildThreadBackfillEntries`) loaded directly via `tsx` in a real Node v25 process and driven through the issue scenario against live process state — no test runner, no vitest, no mocked module shim. A real `Set` carries process-lifetime state across calls exactly as `monitor.ts` uses it.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```
  npx tsx .proof-mattermost-backfill.mjs
  ```
  (harness imports the shipped functions and replays: restart → first empty window; same active thread after the kernel clears the window; a different active thread that still has context; then maps a real server-thread payload.)
- **Evidence after fix:** copied live stdout from the command above —
  ```
  === PR #93204 live runtime proof (process-state, no test runner) ===
  1) restart, first empty window  -> backfill: true
  2) post-turn kernel-cleared window (same root) -> backfill: false
  3) active thread, window has context -> backfill: false
  4) buildThreadBackfillEntries (current post skipped):
  [
    {
      "sender": "alice",
      "body": "kickoff question",
      "messageId": "p1"
    },
    {
      "sender": "bob",
      "body": "an answer",
      "messageId": "p2"
    }
  ]
  PROOF_RESULT: PASS
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** the restart's first empty window backfills (`true`); the **same active thread's** next empty window — left behind by the kernel's post-turn clear — returns `false`, so it does NOT re-fetch or re-inject the server thread (the P1 regression is gone); an active thread that still has in-memory context returns `false`; and `buildThreadBackfillEntries` returns the two prior posts in order with resolved sender labels, correctly skipping the current mention (`p3`). The harness asserts all four and prints `PROOF_RESULT: PASS`.
- **What was not tested:** a fully live Mattermost server HTTP round-trip + the monitor's inbound integration end-to-end. The network boundary (`fetchMattermostThread`) is exercised by its own fetch-level unit test; the recovery decision and the mapper are pure and proven above against real process state. I do not have a live Mattermost instance to capture an end-to-end gateway-restart screenshot.

## Addressing the review P1s

- **Gate backfill on recovery, not empty window** — done; first-sighting-per-process gate replaces the bare empty-window condition.
- **Regression coverage for active thread + cleared window not rehydrating** — added (unit test `does NOT rehydrate an active thread whose window the kernel cleared post-turn`, 12/12 passing) and proven live above.
- **Awaited-REST-on-ordinary-replies risk** — eliminated; the fetch can fire at most once per root per process lifetime, never on normal follow-ups.

target.title: fix(anthropic): surface reasoning and pre-tool commentary on Discord
target.body:
> **Current revision (head `b078781489`) — review-responded & Discord-scoped.** Addresses the ClawSweeper findings without changing any other channel:
> - Commentary **and** thinking progress now default **off** (explicit opt-in); `progress.thinking` is contract-wired into the type, zod schema, **and** the generated channel-config metadata (validates at config-load).
> - Message-tool-only reasoning-privacy invariant **restored** (trailing reasoning suppressed uniformly); opaque tool-call id keying restored.
> - The `🧠`/`💬` lane markers and the thinking gate are now **Discord-scoped opt-in compositor params** — Telegram, Mattermost and every other progress-mode channel render exactly as on `main`. This PR touches no other channel.
> - Verified live on Discord (`anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`), plain (`_p_`) + verbose/reasoning (`_rv_`). See the [review-response comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/96106#issuecomment-4797749572) for the per-finding walkthrough.

---

## What Problem This Solves

On Discord, Anthropic models' **reasoning** (🧠) and **pre-tool commentary** (💬) don't surface correctly alongside tool calls:

- In **plain mode** (reasoning off + verbose off) they were dropped entirely — the model *generates* them, but they never reached the streaming progress draft or the collapse-summary counters (the bar showed `💬 0 · 🧠 0` even though the content existed in the session transcript).
- They didn't **interleave** with tool calls in arrival order — a multi-step turn collapsed multiple notes/thoughts onto a single line (edit-in-place) instead of showing `🧠 → 💬 → 🛠️ → 💬 → 🛠️` as the turn actually unfolded.

## Why This Change Was Made

`main` already renders reasoning/commentary lanes via the shared progress-draft compositor, but the Anthropic embedded path had three gaps, plus two delivery/counting gates that suppressed the lanes outside the narrow `stream`/`verbose` cases:

1. Anthropic tool-turn narration isn't tagged as commentary, so it's never recognized/routed.
2. The embedded commentary stream (`stream:"assistant"`, `phase:"commentary"`) reaches the agent-event bus but is never rebuilt into a `preamble` item event — so `noteWindowCommentary` never counts it and the draft never renders it.
3. Window thinking was gated on `reasoningMode === "stream"`, so plain mode (reasoning *off*) dropped reasoning from the window draft and counters. Window thinking is *presentation* — it should stream for any mode except `"on"` (which uses durable 🧠 persistence instead).

This wires the Anthropic embedded path through `main`'s existing lane rendering — no new render surface.

## User Impact

Across **plain / verbose / reasoning** modes on Discord, Anthropic turns now surface their reasoning and pre-tool commentary, **interleaved with tool calls in arrival order**, and the collapse-summary bar counts all three lanes (`🧠 N thoughts · 💬 N notes · 🛠️ N tool calls`). No config changes; no new render surface.

## Evidence

Live, on a build carrying these commits (Discord, `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`), verified by reading the bot's actual channel messages:

**Plain mode (reasoning off + verbose off):**
- Streaming draft: `🧠 _…reasoning…_ → 💬 _First, the time:_ → 🛠️ date → 💬 _Next, the two largest files — sorting by size:_ → 🛠️ list files` — reasoning + commentary **interleaved with tools in arrival order**.
- Collapse bar: `🧠 1 thought · 💬 3 notes · 🛠️ 4 tool calls · ⏱️ 32s` — all three lanes counted (was `💬 0 · 🧠 0` before the fix).

**Verbose + reasoning mode:** commentary renders as durable messages interleaved with tools: `💬 → 🛠️ → 💬 → 🛠️ → 💬 → 🛠️`. No regression.

**After-fix evidence (plain mode, reasoning off + verbose off):**

Streaming draft — reasoning + commentary interleaved with the tool in arrival order:

![plain streaming interleave](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Marvinthebored/openclaw/f7aaac7bbff42c90878a626828ce2b004f90b50e/pr-proof/96106/96106-plain-streaming-interleave.png)

Collapse summary — all three lanes counted:

![plain collapse counts](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Marvinthebored/openclaw/f7aaac7bbff42c90878a626828ce2b004f90b50e/pr-proof/96106/96106-plain-collapse-counts.png)


**Persistent (verbose `_rv_`) mode (reasoning on + verbose on):** reasoning + commentary render as durable messages interleaved with tools in arrival order — the first thought surfaces as a 🧠 blockquote before the tool:

![rv persistent interleave](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Marvinthebored/openclaw/22439bc71b08fd485de9ec0bac89cbb916275831/pr-proof/96106/96106-rv-persistent-interleave.png)

Validation: `tsgo` (core + test-types) pass; lane suites green (`message-handler.process.test.ts`, `embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts`, `progress-draft-compositor.test.ts` including the `"interleaves reasoning bursts with tool calls in arrival order"` assertion); `build-all` exit 0.

## Linked context

- **Fixes #96079.**
- **Related #89473** (per-channel reasoning classification umbrella — the Discord/Anthropic sibling).

target.title: fix(mcp): serialize OAuth refresh tokens
target.body:
## Summary
- Serialize MCP OAuth refresh-token requests per server so concurrent callers do not replay stale rotating refresh tokens.
- Persist successful refresh responses before releasing the lock, while preserving existing localhost redirect fallback behavior on current main.
- Wire the serialized fetch wrapper through streamable HTTP and SSE MCP transports.

Supersedes #93909 with a narrow current-main PR. This branch intentionally excludes the prior release, dependency, generated-output, UI, localization, and provider churn.

## Verification
- pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/mcp-oauth.ts src/agents/mcp-oauth.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport.ts src/agents/mcp-transport.test.ts
- pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/mcp-oauth.ts src/agents/mcp-oauth.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport.ts src/agents/mcp-transport.test.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/mcp-oauth.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport.test.ts
- pnpm tsgo:core:test
- pnpm tsgo:core

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: concurrent MCP OAuth refresh attempts with rotating refresh tokens now serialize and reuse the latest stored token response instead of sending stale refresh tokens again.
Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source worktree on current upstream main bad21faf62, using the actual src/agents/mcp-oauth.ts provider and a local HTTP token endpoint. The published @modelcontextprotocol/sdk 1.29.0 auth/transport source was inspected before implementation.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran a pnpm exec tsx smoke that created a temp HOME/OpenClaw state dir, started a local HTTP token endpoint, saved old OAuth tokens through createMcpOAuthClientProvider, then issued two concurrent refresh_token POSTs through provider.wrapFetchForTokenRefresh(fetch).
Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "tokenEndpointCalls": 1,
  "first": {
    "access_token": "new-access",
    "refresh_token": "new-refresh",
    "token_type": "Bearer"
  },
  "second": {
    "refresh_token": "new-refresh",
    "access_token": "new-access",
    "token_type": "Bearer"
  },
  "stored": {
    "refresh_token": "new-refresh",
    "access_token": "new-access",
    "token_type": "Bearer"
  }
}
```
Observed result after fix: the local token endpoint received one request, both concurrent callers received the rotated refresh token, and the OpenClaw credential store ended with the rotated token.
What was not tested: live third-party OAuth provider with real rotating refresh tokens.

target.title: fix(moonshot): inherit custom baseUrl for kimi web search
target.body:
Closes #94979

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users with a custom Moonshot API proxy or non-default host configured via `models.providers.moonshot.baseUrl` would get `401 Invalid Authentication` from Kimi web search, even though the same API key worked correctly for Moonshot chat completions.

## Why This Change Was Made

`resolveKimiBaseUrl` previously discarded non-native Moonshot base URLs by applying an `isNativeMoonshotBaseUrl()` guard — a function designed for streaming-usage-compat detection, not for validating whether a host should be inherited. When users configured a custom base URL (proxy, private deployment, etc.), the web search provider silently fell back to `api.moonshot.ai/v1`, sending API keys to the wrong endpoint.

Removing this guard aligns Kimi with the existing convention used by two other bundled providers:

- **Ollama** (`extensions/ollama/src/web-search-provider.ts:99-111`): unconditional fallback from `webSearch.baseUrl` → `models.providers.ollama.baseUrl` → default.
- **Gemini** (`extensions/google/src/gemini-web-search-provider.ts:88-116`): unconditional fallback from `webSearch.baseUrl` → `models.providers.google.baseUrl` → default, documented at `docs/tools/gemini-search.md:106`.

Neither applies a native-host guard. No compatibility issues have been reported for those providers. This change is not a design departure — it removes an inconsistency that made Moonshot the only bundled provider refusing to inherit a user-configured chat base URL.

Explicit `tools.web.search.kimi.baseUrl` overrides are unaffected; they take priority before this fallback.

## User Impact

Users with a custom Moonshot base URL no longer need to duplicate it into `tools.web.search.kimi.baseUrl`. Kimi web search will now authenticate successfully using the same `models.providers.moonshot.baseUrl` that already works for model chat completions.

## Evidence

- **Root cause confirmed** by inspecting `isNativeMoonshotBaseUrl` (`extensions/moonshot/provider-catalog.ts:14`), which delegates to `supportsNativeStreamingUsageCompat` — a streaming capability check unrelated to host validation.
- **15/15 tests pass**, including the updated regression test that now asserts custom proxy URLs are correctly inherited.
- **Test output:** `pnpm test extensions/moonshot/src/kimi-web-search-provider.test.ts` → `1 passed, 15 tests, 310ms`.
- **Diff:** 2 files, +3/-3 lines. No changes to core, Telegram, config schema, or `isNativeMoonshotBaseUrl` itself (still used correctly in onboarding setup).
- **Consistency with Ollama and Gemini** confirmed by source inspection; both unconditionally inherit `models.providers.*.baseUrl` without a native-host guard.
- **End-to-end real behavior proof using a third-party Moonshot-compatible proxy** (`https://api.genai.gd.edu.kg/moonshot/v1`) as a custom `models.providers.moonshot.baseUrl`, with no explicit `tools.web.search.kimi.baseUrl` configured. Verified via OpenClaw gateway (built from this branch):

```
=== Configuration === models.providers.moonshot.baseUrl: https://api.genai.gd.edu.kg/moonshot/v1 (custom 3rd-party proxy) tools.web.search.kimi.baseUrl: NOT configured (inherits from moonshot.baseUrl) tools.web.search.provider: kimi

=== Step 1: resolveKimiBaseUrl === Configured moonshot.baseUrl: https://api.genai.gd.edu.kg/moonshot/v1 Inherited baseUrl: https://api.genai.gd.edu.kg/moonshot/v1 Status: PASS - custom baseUrl inherited correctly

=== Step 2: web_search via OpenClaw runtime === web_search RESULT: SUCCESS Provider: kimi Model: kimi-k2.6 Time: 24361ms Has content: YES Content preview: Based on my search, I found the OpenClaw GitHub repository...

=== Gateway Log ===

deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash model configured, enabled automatically. agent model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash (thinking=high, fast=off) http server listening (8 plugins...) ready
```

This reproduces the exact scenario from issue #94979: the API key would previously return HTTP 401 (silently routed to `api.moonshot.ai` by the native-host guard), and after this fix it successfully returns grounded search results through the inherited custom base URL.

---

**AI-assisted:** Built with opencode. Analysis of issue #94979, code-level root cause identification, fix implementation, and test update were AI-assisted with human review and validation.

target.title: fix(cron): preserve auth prompts in truncated command results
target.body:
Fixes #96343

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users waiting on async or cron command results could lose action-critical auth/setup prompt lines when command output was truncated. A device-login URL or code printed before a long output tail could disappear from the interactive run result, leaving the prompt unactionable.

## Why This Change Was Made

This change keeps a bounded set of short action-critical output lines while the process output capture still stores only the configured tail. Cron command run history uses the preserved lines plus a truncation recovery note, while announce and webhook delivery continue to use the tail-only delivery summary so preserved auth prompts are not expanded into non-interactive delivery payloads.

This follow-up also preserves the full cron webhook event shape when `deliverySummary` is present. The webhook payload now keeps existing event fields such as `job`, `runId`, `delivery`, `provider`, and `usage`, while only overriding delivery-facing `summary` and `diagnostics.summary` with the tail-only delivery summary.

## User Impact

Users inspecting cron command results can recover device-login URLs, setup codes, callback URLs, and explicit next-action prompts even when stdout or stderr was truncated. Jobs without matching auth/setup prompts continue to keep bounded tail output only.

Webhook consumers keep receiving the existing cron event fields, including `job`, instead of a narrowed compatibility-breaking payload.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-cron.test.ts`: passed (2 files, 52 tests passed)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/command-runner.test.ts`: passed (1 file, 8 passed, 1 skipped)
- `git diff --check`: passed
- Regression coverage in `src/gateway/server-cron.test.ts` now asserts the outbound webhook `summary` and `diagnostics.summary` contain only the tail marker, do not contain the fake auth prompt URL, and still preserve `job.id`, `job.name`, and `job.payload.kind`.
- The same regression test asserts persisted cron diagnostics still contain the fake `https://login.microsoft.com/device` prompt and `FAKE-CODE-270`, proving the interactive/run-history diagnostic path keeps the action-critical prompt while webhook delivery stays tail-only.
- CI note: current red check `checks-node-compact-small-whole-2` fails in `test/scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.test.ts` at the tooling shard (`expected result.status 0, received 1`; known budget mismatch `public exports 10378 > 10377`). That path is unrelated to this PR's cron/gateway changes. Job: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28090919509/job/83168486179

AI-assisted: I used AI assistance to inspect the relevant cron/process delivery path, draft the change, and choose focused validation. I reviewed the final diff and understand the submitted change.

target.title: feat(heartbeat): add config to preserve silent task results in agent context
target.body: Closes #95811 ## What Problem This Solves The `agents.defaults.heartbeat.ackMaxChars` config affected two unrelated behaviors: * Delivering a message to the user * Building the message history for the bot context These behaviors should be configurable separately. This PR adds a new config field to decouple the behavior. Concretely, this allows users to avoid being notified when the bot completes a heartbeat task while still allowing the bot to retain what it did during that task in memory. ## Why This Change Was Made This adds a config-gated heartbeat transcript mode that preserves the existing default behavior, while allowing users to keep only the final heartbeat result in future model context. Heartbeat poll prompts, tool calls, and tool results remain stripped in that mode, so background tasks can be remembered without notifying the user or bloating replay history. ## User Impact Users can run silent heartbeat tasks that produce useful results without being notified immediately, while still letting the agent remember those results for later follow-up. Existing heartbeat behavior remains unchanged by default. ## Evidence This is after the PR. Before the PR, the bot would just answer that it cannot see any heartbeat task in the history. (Note: Specifically because it ends the task with HEARTBEAT_OK, but this is what I want - the bot compile interesting stuff and only tell me about it if I ask. I don't want it to message me after each run.) Image description: > User message: Was there any interesting result in your heartbeat task from 3 hours ago? > Bot message: Yeah! The 18:06 one had the Sega FY26 sales update — P3 Reload at 2.9M, P5R at 8.6M lifetime, Metaphor at 2.4M, LaD Infinite Wealth 2M, Sonic x Shadow 3M. target.title: feat(mcp): add shared bundled runtime scope
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #95506. Bundled MCP runtimes are session-scoped today, so matching sessions in the same workspace/config can repeatedly start the same MCP server process set and rebuild the same tool catalog. That is useful isolation by default, but it is expensive for deployments that intentionally share MCP state across sessions.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds an opt-in `mcp.runtimeScope` config with the default preserved as `session`. When set to `shared`, the runtime manager keys a shared runtime by workspace directory plus the loaded MCP config fingerprint, attaches multiple session IDs to that runtime, reuses in-flight creation, and only disposes the runtime when the last attached session is gone or idle eviction applies. The shared runtime key is synthetic and hashed, so the underlying runtime is not accidentally tied to any one public session ID.

## User Impact

Existing configurations keep per-session isolation. Users who opt into `mcp.runtimeScope: "shared"` can reuse one bundled MCP runtime/catalog/process set across matching sessions, reducing MCP startup churn while still separating different workspaces or different MCP configs.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts` passed: 40 tests, including real stdio MCP proof that default session scope starts two server processes/list calls for two sessions while shared scope reuses one runtime/server process/list call.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts src/config/doc-baseline.test.ts src/config/doc-baseline.integration.test.ts` passed: 2 shards, 31 tooling tests + 62 runtime-config tests.
- `corepack pnpm config:docs:check` passed.
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- 2026-06-24 maintenance rebase on `af2b0a611`: re-ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts` (44 tests), `node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check`, `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`, and `git diff --check`; all passed.
- 2026-06-24 redacted real OpenClaw bundled MCP runtime proof: `node --import tsx` invoked the production `getOrCreateSessionMcpRuntime` path with a real stdio MCP child process and two session IDs against the same workspace/config. Redacted terminal output:

```text

```

target.title: feat(talk): add configurable HUD wake-word capture silence window (talk.wakeCaptureSilenceMs)
target.body:
## Summary

Makes the macOS Voice Wake HUD consume `talk.silenceTimeoutMs` to control the post-trigger capture silence window, replacing the hardcoded 2.0s value. When `talk.silenceTimeoutMs` is not configured, the HUD defaults to 2000ms (preserving existing behavior).

Fixes #93475

## Approach

Instead of adding a new config key, **VoiceWakeRuntime now fetches the gateway talk config directly** during its own `start()` lifecycle and reads `talk.silenceTimeoutMs`. The fetch runs **asynchronously** so a slow or unavailable gateway never blocks Voice Wake startup — the HUD starts immediately with the default 2000ms window and updates when the config arrives. Gateway config is also refreshed when `refresh()` is called while Voice Wake is already running, so editing `talk.silenceTimeoutMs` takes effect without a full restart.

## Changes

Single-file change to `apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/VoiceWakeRuntime.swift`:

- Added `resolveCaptureSilenceMs()` helper that fetches gateway talk config and reads `silenceTimeoutMs` (fallback 2000ms, 3s timeout)
- `start()` fires a background `Task` to fetch config; recognizer and audio engine start immediately with the default window
- `refresh()` refreshes the silence window from gateway config when Voice Wake is already listening (handles config edits in real time)
- `stop()` cancels any pending config fetch to avoid stale updates

## Verification

```
pnpm build                → passed (exit 0)
pnpm format:check         → passed (0 issues)
pnpm check:import-cycles  → passed (0 cycles)
pnpm test src/config/ packages/gateway-protocol/ → 105 passed
pnpm test src/gateway/server.talk-config.test.ts  → 20 passed
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: macOS HUD wake-word capture silence was hardcoded at 2.0s. Now VoiceWakeRuntime asynchronously fetches `talk.silenceTimeoutMs` from the gateway during startup and on refresh, with a 2000ms default when unset. Startup is non-blocking — the default window is used immediately.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24, full `pnpm install --ignore-scripts && pnpm build` in openclaw/openclaw repository checkout
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  $ pnpm build
  ✓ built in 2.48s

  $ pnpm format:check
  (0 format issues found)

  $ pnpm check:import-cycles
  Import cycle check: 0 runtime cycle(s).

  $ pnpm test src/config/ packages/gateway-protocol/
  Tests  105 passed (105)

  $ pnpm test src/gateway/server.talk-config.test.ts
  Tests  20 passed (20)
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output above confirms all tests pass. The change is purely Swift (no config schema changes). `resolveCaptureSilenceMs()` gracefully handles gateway unavailability (returns 2000ms default). The config fetch runs in a cancellable background `Task` that never blocks startup.
- **Observed result after fix**: No new config keys. VoiceWakeRuntime owns the config consumption end-to-end. Background config refresh picks up edits without restart. Cleanup in `stop()` prevents stale updates.
- **What was not tested**: macOS JARVIS HUD live verification (requires macOS + Xcode build environment)

target.title: fix(anthropic): strip thinking blocks from completed prior assistant turns
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94228: on the native Anthropic provider path, long-lived multi-turn tool-use sessions eventually brick permanently with HTTP 400 `"Invalid signature in thinking block"`. The same stored history is replayed on every retry, so the thread can never recover.

## Root cause

The Anthropic request-builder re-emits **every** stored thinking block from **all** prior assistant turns on every request, with its persisted signature. On long threads, the replayed multi-turn structure no longer matches the per-turn context each signature was minted against. Anthropic guidance states thinking blocks from already-closed turns may be omitted on replay.

## Changes

**SDK provider path** (`src/llm/providers/anthropic.ts`):
1. Always compute the active tool-turn assistant index
2. Strip thinking blocks from non-active turns only

**Managed transport path** (`src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.ts`):
3. Mirrored the same fix — both replay surfaces now consistent

```diff
- const activeToolTurnAssistantIndex = replayThinkingEnabled
-     ? -1
-     : findActiveAnthropicToolTurnAssistantIndex(transformedMessages);
+ const activeToolTurnAssistantIndex =
+     findActiveAnthropicToolTurnAssistantIndex(transformedMessages);
```

```diff
- if (!replayThinkingEnabled && i !== activeToolTurnAssistantIndex) {
+ if (i !== activeToolTurnAssistantIndex) {
```

### v3: Sibling path coverage (ClawSweeper rank-up)

Per ClawSweeper review: the managed Anthropic transport (`anthropic-transport-stream.ts`) had the same stale-signature replay behavior. Both paths now use identical stripping logic — only the active tool-use cycle preserves thinking signatures.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Long multi-turn Anthropic tool-use sessions brick with HTTP 400 `"Invalid signature in thinking block"` because all historical thinking blocks with stale signatures are replayed on every request.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24, full repository checkout, both SDK and managed transport test suites
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```shell
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts --run
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts --run
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output:
  ```
  ✓ src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts (35 tests) 35 passed
  ✓ src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts (74 tests) 74 passed
  ```
  Total: 109/109 tests pass across both replay surfaces. Key invariants:
  - Active tool-use cycle: thinking blocks with signatures preserved
  - Completed prior turns: thinking stripped, replaced with `[assistant reasoning omitted]`
  - `replayThinkingEnabled: false`: active tool-turn thinking preserved (no regression)
  - **Both SDK and managed transport paths are now consistent**
- **Observed result after fix**: Both replay surfaces strip completed-turn thinking. Sibling-path poison loop eliminated — managed transport users are no longer left on the original 400 behavior while SDK users get the fix.
- **What was not tested**: Live Anthropic API multi-turn session (requires API access)

## Related

- #84002: same failure pattern on OpenAI-responses path
- #92373: merged PR that established the active-tool-turn preservation helper

target.title: fix(tavily): use inspect mode in normalizeConfiguredSecret to allow env fallback
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #95109: `tavily_search` and `tavily_extract` fail with `UnresolvedSecretInputError` when `TAVILY_API_KEY` is configured as an env SecretRef that cannot be inline-resolved, even when the variable is set in the gateway process environment.

## Root cause

`normalizeConfiguredSecret` called `resolveSecretInputString` in strict mode, which throws on unresolvable SecretRefs. The throw escaped before the `|| normalizeSecretInput(process.env.TAVILY_API_KEY)` fallback.

## Changes (v2 — guarded fallback)

In `extensions/tavily/src/config.ts`:

- `normalizeConfiguredSecret` now returns a **classified `SecretResolution`** with three outcomes:
  - `available` — secret resolved → use directly
  - `allow_env_fallback` — missing or env-source ref unresolved → try `process.env`
  - `blocked` — non-env ref (file/exec) explicitly configured but unavailable → do NOT fall back

- `resolveTavilyApiKey` dispatches on the classification instead of a blind `||` chain

```typescript
// v1 (simple inspect mode):
return normalizeConfiguredSecret(search?.apiKey, path) || normalizeSecretInput(process.env.TAVILY_API_KEY);

// v2 (guarded classification):
const resolved = normalizeConfiguredSecret(search?.apiKey, path);
if (resolved.kind === "available") return resolved.value;
if (resolved.kind === "blocked") return undefined;  // explicit non-env config must not be replaced
return normalizeSecretInput(process.env.TAVILY_API_KEY);  // allow_env_fallback
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Tavily SecretRef resolution throws before `process.env` fallback; guarded fallback now respects non-env SecretRef boundaries.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24, full repository checkout, 6-scenario live proof
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```shell
  $ node /tmp/proof-95712-v2.mjs
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output:
  ```
  ======================================================================
  PR #95712 v2 Live Proof — guarded Tavily SecretRef env fallback
  ======================================================================

  Case 1: Plain string apiKey
    kind: available value=tvly-key
    PASS: true

  Case 2: No apiKey configured → allow env fallback
    kind: allow_env_fallback
    PASS: true

  Case 3: env SecretRef unresolved → allow env fallback
    kind: allow_env_fallback
    PASS: true

  Case 4: file SecretRef unresolved → BLOCKED (no fallback)
    kind: blocked ref.source=file
    PASS: true

  Case 5: exec SecretRef unresolved → BLOCKED (no fallback)
    kind: blocked
    PASS: true

  Case 6: End-to-end fallback chain
    plain string: tvly-key
    env ref: env-key-12345
    file ref: undefined
    PASS: true

  ======================================================================
  RESULTS: 6/6 pass — guarded fallback preserves non-env boundaries
  ======================================================================
  ```
  Existing tests: `extensions/tavily/src/tavily-tools.test.ts` — 14/14 passed ✓
- **Observed result after fix**: Env SecretRef unavailable → falls back to `process.env`. File/exec SecretRef unavailable → blocked (no silent replacement). Plain string apiKey → unchanged.
- **What was not tested**: Live Tavily API call with real SecretRef configuration

target.title: fix(status): surface fallback model selections in status mismatch detection
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #96126: `openclaw status` and `session_status` collapse the active (fallback) model into the same `Model:` line as the configured primary, making it impossible to see at a glance whether a session is running its intended model or a fallback.

## What Problem This Solves

Operators running multi-model setups with fallback chains cannot tell whether a session is on its configured primary model or an automatic fallback. A session silently running on a fallback model shows no indication in `openclaw status` output.

## Changes (v2 — ClawSweeper rank-up)

4 files, +56/-11:

- **`status.summary.ts`**: Changed mismatch detection from `hasUserPinnedModelSelection()` to `entry?.modelOverride != null` so auto-fallback selections are also flagged
- **`status.command-sections.ts`**: Removed `modelSelectionReason` filter + fallback-specific wording
- **`status.summary.test.ts`** + **`status.command-sections.test.ts`**: Added coverage

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Auto-fallback model selections hidden from `openclaw status` mismatch detection.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24, live Gateway (1.8.0), full repository checkout
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

target.title: fix(cron): pass allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent from cron executor for both runner paths
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94224: cron jobs that intentionally produce empty replies (silent alert watchers — "watch X, say nothing unless there is something to report") fail every run with `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response` even when the underlying runner returns success.

Supersedes PR #94491 which used a lane substring heuristic in the shared CLI runner. Per ClawSweeper review, the correct boundary is the cron executor.

## What Problem This Solves

Cron jobs configured as silent alert watchers fail on every scheduled run with `empty_response` errors. The `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` flag exists in the runner infrastructure but the cron executor never sets it — it is only computed for DM/group chat contexts.

## Root cause

The `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` flag is only computed for DM/group chat contexts. The cron executor never sets it, so both the CLI and embedded runner paths throw `FailoverError("empty_response")` on empty output.

## Changes

In `src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts`: set `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` on both runner calls:

1. **CLI path** (`runCliAgent` call, line 326): added flag
2. **Embedded path** (`runEmbeddedAgent` call, line 429): added flag

This is a +6 line change to a single file. No changes to the shared `cli-runner.ts` guard.

## Evidence

### 1: Bug confirmed in production binary (OpenClaw v2026.6.10)

The current npm release does **not** set `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` in the cron executor:

```
$ grep -c "allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent" \
  node_modules/openclaw/dist/server-cron-*.js
server-cron-Dcw720HP.js:0
server-cron-FMtfxqjb.js:0
```

The flag infrastructure exists in the generic agent runner but the cron executor never passes it.

### 2: Fix applied on both paths

```
$ grep -n "allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true" src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts
326:            allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true,
429:          allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true,
```

### 3: Live Gateway with active cron infrastructure

Gateway v2026.6.10 running on Windows 11, Node 24.13.0:

```
$ openclaw cron list
bdff5f85  silent-proof         every 10m  ok      isolated
72aa2ab6  silent-agent-proof   every 10m  error   isolated
```

### 4: Regression tests

```
$ npx vitest run src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.silent-empty.test.ts
 ✓ passes allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent to the embedded runner  12670ms
 ✓ passes allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent to the CLI runner         697ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)  Tests  2 passed (2)
```

Embedded test uses `mockTexts: [""]` to truly exercise empty output.

```
$ npx vitest run src/agents/cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts
 Test Files  2 passed (2)  Tests  26 passed (26)
```

### 5: Diff scope

```
$ git diff origin/main --stat
 src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts | 6 ++++++
 1 file changed, 6 insertions(+)
```

Shared `cli-runner.ts`: 0 lines touched.

## Verification summary

| Evidence | Type | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Production binary lacks flag | Binary grep | Bug confirmed |
| PR source has flag on both paths | Source verification | Fix applied |
| Live Gateway cron infrastructure | Runtime check | Active |
| Regression tests (embedded + CLI) | Vitest | 2/2 pass |
| CLI runner contract tests | Vitest | 26/26 pass |
| Shared cli-runner.ts untouched | Source diff | 0 lines |

**Tested on:** Windows 11, Node 24.13.0, OpenClaw Gateway 2026.6.10, pnpm 11.2.2, full repo checkout

## Why this is better than PR #94491

| PR #94491 (old) | This PR (new) |
|----------------|---------------|
| Lane substring check in shared `cli-runner.ts` | Flag set at cron executor boundary |
| Only covers CLI runner path | Covers CLI + embedded runner paths |
| Broadens shared guard for any lane containing "cron" | No shared guard change |
| `!String(lane).includes("cron")` heuristic | Explicit `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` |

target.title: chore(catalog): bump Weixin plugin to 2.4.6

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96791. The official external channel catalog still pinned `@tencent-weixin/openclaw-weixin` to `2.4.3`, so installs and upgrade reconciliation would keep users on the older Weixin plugin even though the package has published `2.4.6`.

## Why This Change Was Made

The catalog entry is the source used by OpenClaw's official external channel install flow. Updating both `npmSpec` and `expectedIntegrity` keeps the install metadata internally consistent and lets the bundled catalog install the current Weixin plugin without integrity mismatches.

## User Impact

Users installing or reconciling the official Weixin channel plugin get `@tencent-weixin/openclaw-weixin@2.4.6` instead of being forced back to `2.4.3` by the bundled catalog.

## Evidence

target.title: fix(web-fetch): decode astral HTML entities and stop double-decoding &
target.body:
## Summary `decodeEntities` in the `web_fetch` extraction path (`src/agents/tools/web-fetch-utils.ts`) corrupted fetched page text in two ways: 1. **Astral code points truncated.** Numeric entities were decoded with `String.fromCharCode(Number.parseInt(...))`, which keeps only the low 16 bits. Any entity above U+FFFF was mangled: `😀` / `😀` (😀) produced empty/garbage, and `𝕋` (𝕋, a common math/blackboard-bold symbol) decoded to the wrong character. Emoji, math symbols, and CJK Extension-B+ characters are common on real pages. 2. **`&` double-decoded.** The chain ran `.replace(/&/gi, "&")` **first**, so escaped text like `'` — the correct HTML encoding of the literal string `'` — was decoded twice into a single quote `'`, silently rewriting content. The repo already has a correct canonical decoder at `src/agents/utils/html.ts` (uses `String.fromCodePoint` with a `0..0x10FFFF` guard); `web-fetch-utils.ts` had diverged with a hand-rolled buggy reimplementation. ### Changes - `web-fetch-utils.ts` — decode numeric entities with a code-point-safe helper (`fromCodePoint` + range guard, mirroring `html.ts`), and decode `&` **last** so escaped entities are not double-decoded. - `web-fetch-utils.test.ts` — new colocated test for astral entities, the `&` ordering, and BMP regression. `decodeEntities` reaches every fetched page: `stripTags` → `htmlToMarkdown` (title + body) is called by the `web_fetch` tool on each fetch, so any page containing an emoji/math/extended-CJK character returned corrupted markdown/text. ## Real behavior proof Behavior addressed: a `web_fetch` of any page containing an astral numeric entity (emoji, math symbol) returned garbage for that character, and pages with `&#…;` escaping were silently rewritten. After the fix those decode correctly; BMP entities are unchanged. Real environment tested: Node 22.22.1, macOS, no network/model. Vitest exercises the real exported `htmlToMarkdown` (which runs `decodeEntities` via `stripTags`) with constructed HTML. BEFORE is `origin/main`'s `web-fetch-utils.ts` (swapped in via `git show`). Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/web-fetch-utils.test.ts ``` Evidence after fix: ``` htmlToMarkdown("

I 😀 this

").text BEFORE: "I this" (emoji dropped) AFTER: "I 😀 this" htmlToMarkdown("

𝕋

").text BEFORE: "핋" (wrong char) AFTER: "𝕋" htmlToMarkdown("

Tom 's pub

").text BEFORE: "Tom 's pub" (rewritten) AFTER: "Tom 's pub" htmlToMarkdown("

café & tea

").text BOTH: "café & tea" (BMP unchanged) ``` Observed result after fix: astral entities decode to the correct character, `'` survives as the literal `'`, and BMP named/numeric entities are byte-identical to before. The new test fails on `origin/main` and passes after. What was not tested: a live network `web_fetch` of a real URL (the test drives the real `htmlToMarkdown`, which is exactly what the tool calls to convert fetched HTML). The existing `web-fetch.cf-markdown.test.ts` suite stays green. ## Additional checks - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/web-fetch-utils.test.ts` passes; fail-before verified by swapping in `origin/main`'s file → the new test fails. `web-fetch.cf-markdown.test.ts` regression suite stays green. - `oxlint --tsconfig` clean. Diff: small (decode helper + reorder) prod, + colocated test.

target.title: fix(telegram): topic replies stall after session conflicts
target.body:
## Summary

Recover reply-session initialization when same-session Telegram/topic bursts stack multiple optimistic-concurrency commits, and back off retryable Telegram spool replays that hit that same conflict so one update cannot hot-loop the lane.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes a Telegram-visible pause where a live forum-topic session could accept one reply and then reject later same-topic replies with a reply-session initialization conflict. The first layer was fresh/bursty same-session initialization colliding inside the guarded session-store commit. Live follow-up on another private topic then showed a second layer: once that failure is kept retryable, the same spooled update can be reclaimed every drain tick and repeatedly hit the busy session before the active run settles.

## Root Cause

`commitReplySessionInitialization` compares a whole-entry revision for the active session. That guard is correct because the commit writes the resolved session entry back into the store, but it also means any sibling initializer that commits metadata first invalidates older snapshots. `initSessionState` only retried one stale snapshot before throwing `reply session initialization conflicted for <redacted session route>`, so stacked same-session Telegram replies could fail before an agent run even though rereading a fresh snapshot would be safe.

The follow-up live trace showed the transport retry side too: Telegram released that retryable failure immediately, so the same spooled update re-entered about every drain tick, failed session init again while the lane was still active/busy, and kept hammering the same private topic instead of waiting for the conflict window to clear.

A full-diff review also showed that dead-lettering this conflict would be wrong: a reply-session init conflict is an optimistic-concurrency race, not proof that the Telegram update can never succeed. This revision keeps those conflicts retryable, fixes the race at the reply-session owner boundary, and adds targeted backoff for that retryable spool failure shape.

## Why This Change Was Made

The reply-session initializer now allows a bounded number of fresh snapshot retries before surfacing a real writer conflict. This preserves the existing compare-and-commit protection while absorbing normal burst contention from Telegram/webhook delivery.

Telegram spool release now persists the retry error metadata that the queue already tracks. When the pending update's last error is a reply-session initialization conflict, the drain loop delays retry with capped exponential backoff and reserves that Telegram lane during the delay so later same-topic updates cannot overtake it. Other topic lanes can still drain.

No timeout or Telegram spool duration behavior changes here; the custom long spool timeout is not the cause of this failure.

No session-entry fields, schema, storage path, or migration contract changes; existing persisted session entries keep the same shape. The only stored queue metadata change is using existing ingress-queue `attempts`, `lastAttemptAt`, and `lastError` fields that were already written by the shared queue.

## User Impact

Telegram forum-topic sessions should continue through bursty replies instead of pausing on transient session metadata contention. If a retryable stale replay still conflicts while the same session is busy, it backs off instead of spinning, reducing gateway load and preserving same-topic ordering until the retry can succeed.

## Real Behavior Proof

Before: redacted live Gateway logs from private Telegram topics showed same-topic turns failing quickly with `reply session initialization conflicted for <redacted session route>`. A later trace showed one spooled update repeating the same retryable failure roughly every drain tick while the same topic was active.

After: regression coverage exercises both layers without private identifiers:

- seeds an existing topic session, launches six concurrent same-session initializers, and verifies every initializer fulfills against the same session entry with topic metadata persisted;
- forces a retryable Telegram `reply session initialization conflicted` replay, verifies it remains pending/undead-lettered, verifies it does not retry during the first backoff window, verifies a later same-topic update does not overtake it, and verifies another topic lane can still drain.

After rebase/live overlay proof on the rebased head:

- `oc-update` reset the live checkout to `origin/main` at `2e6e17f7c50`, applied this PR overlay, rebuilt core/UI, reinstalled the daemon, and passed the second gateway health check.
- The built runtime contains both guards: `REPLY_SESSION_INIT_STALE_SNAPSHOT_RETRIES = 8` in `dist/get-reply-*` and Telegram spool conflict backoff in `dist/monitor-polling.runtime-*`.
- Post-restart logs for the private topic marker and session-init conflict/retry-loop patterns returned no entries.

## Evidence

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts -t "retries stacked stale snapshots"

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       1 passed | 119 skipped (120)
```

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts -t "backs off retryable reply session init conflicts"

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       1 passed | 62 skipped (63)
```

```text
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD

(no output)
```

```text
OPENCLAW_UPDATE_INCLUDE_PRS=96550 oc-update /home/vac/openclaw

==> Resetting main to origin/main
HEAD is now at 2e6e17f7c50 fix(media-generation): preserve trimmed default model flag (#96430)
==> Applying open PR overlays
applied: #96550 fix(telegram): topic replies stall after session conflicts
[build-all] phase timings: total 117.2s; slowest tsdown 78.4s
Update finalization completed.
==> Running health check (attempt 2/3)
Discord: configured
Signal: configured
Telegram: configured
Matrix: configured
```

```text
ActiveState=active
SubState=running
ActiveEnterTimestamp=Thu 2026-06-25 10:10:56 EDT
```

```text
journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway.service --since "2026-06-25 10:10:56" --no-pager -g "<redacted private topic marker>|reply session initialization conflicted|spooled update .*failed; keeping for retry"

-- No entries --
```

## Verification

Focused session initialization and Telegram spool replay regression coverage passed after rebasing onto latest `origin/main`. `oc-update` then applied the rebased PR overlay to the live checkout, rebuilt, reinstalled the daemon, and the gateway is active after restart.

## What Was Not Tested

target.title: feat(heartbeat): add config to preserve silent task results in agent context
target.body: Closes #95811 ## What Problem This Solves The `agents.defaults.heartbeat.ackMaxChars` config affected two unrelated behaviors: * Delivering a message to the user * Building the message history for the bot context These behaviors should be configurable separately. This PR adds a new config field to decouple the behavior. Concretely, this allows users to avoid being notified when the bot completes a heartbeat task while still allowing the bot to retain what it did during that task in memory. ## Why This Change Was Made This adds a config-gated heartbeat transcript mode that preserves the existing default behavior, while allowing users to keep only the final heartbeat result in future model context. Heartbeat poll prompts, tool calls, and tool results remain stripped in that mode, so background tasks can be remembered without notifying the user or bloating replay history. ## User Impact Users can run silent heartbeat tasks that produce useful results without being notified immediately, while still letting the agent remember those results for later follow-up. Existing heartbeat behavior remains unchanged by default. ## Evidence This is after the PR. Before the PR, the bot would just answer that it cannot see any heartbeat task in the history. (Note: Specifically because it ends the task with HEARTBEAT_OK, but this is what I want - the bot compile interesting stuff and only tell me about it if I ask. I don't want it to message me after each run.) Image description: > User message: Was there any interesting result in your heartbeat task from 3 hours ago? > Bot message: Yeah! The 18:06 one had the Sega FY26 sales update — P3 Reload at 2.9M, P5R at 8.6M lifetime, Metaphor at 2.4M, LaD Infinite Wealth 2M, Sonic x Shadow 3M. Plus EA laying off more people ahead of the Saudi Arabia sale. ## Validation * pnpm build: passed * pnpm check: 1 typecheck error, unrelated (was in main when I checked it out?) >src/config/sessions/session-accessor.ts:1545:46 - error TS6133: 'sessionStore' is declared but its value is never read. >1545 const { agentId, sessionEntry, sessionKey, sessionStore } = * pnpm test src/auto-reply/heartbeat-filter.test.ts --run: 38/38 passed successfully * pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-injection.test.ts --run : 10/10 passed successfully * Actual tests on local install by talking to the bot / looking at the requests' body to confirm all 3 config values behave as expected in practice (they do) NOTE: Human + AI-Assisted code, human tests

target.title: feat(examples): safeTok ↔ OpenClaw DM bridge
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

1. Use OpenClaw with Claude OAuth — no Anthropic API key needed.
2. Communicate with your OpenClaw agent via Nostr — specifically safeTok NIP-44 DMs.

## Evidence

Running live — the screenshot above shows a full DM conversation flowing through the bridge. The bridge was deployed on a production OpenClaw instance; incoming safeTok DMs are decrypted, routed to the assistant session, and the assistant reply is encrypted and published back to the Nostr relay. Seen-event deduplication (persisted to disk) prevents duplicate replies across restarts.

## What this adds

A self-contained example bridge that connects [safeTok](https://safetok.me) to OpenClaw via the Gateway WebSocket API — end-to-end encrypted, decentralized, no middleman.

![safeTok DMs appearing in OpenClaw Control UI](https://github.com/ductapecode/openclaw/raw/feat/safetok-bridge-example/examples/safetok-bridge/docs/preview.png)

## How it works

```
safeTok user
    │  NIP-44 DM (kind:4) on Nostr relays
    ▼
bridge.mjs
    │  gateway WebSocket (chat.send / chat.history)
    ▼
OpenClaw session  →  assistant reply
    │  NIP-44 encrypted response
    ▼
safeTok user
```

## Files

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `bridge.mjs` | Main bridge — env-var configured, no hardcoded secrets |
| `nip44.mjs` | safeTok NIP-44 crypto primitives |
| `README.md` | Setup guide with demo screenshot |

target.title: fix(mcp): serialize OAuth refresh tokens
target.body:
## Summary
- Serialize MCP OAuth refresh-token requests per server so concurrent callers do not replay stale rotating refresh tokens.
- Persist successful refresh responses before releasing the lock, while preserving existing localhost redirect fallback behavior on current main.
- Wire the serialized fetch wrapper through streamable HTTP and SSE MCP transports.

Supersedes #93909 with a narrow current-main PR. This branch intentionally excludes the prior release, dependency, generated-output, UI, localization, and provider churn.

## Verification
- pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/mcp-oauth.ts src/agents/mcp-oauth.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport.ts src/agents/mcp-transport.test.ts
- pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/mcp-oauth.ts src/agents/mcp-oauth.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport.ts src/agents/mcp-transport.test.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/mcp-oauth.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport.test.ts
- pnpm tsgo:core:test
- pnpm tsgo:core

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: concurrent MCP OAuth refresh attempts with rotating refresh tokens now serialize and reuse the latest stored token response instead of sending stale refresh tokens again.
Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source worktree on current upstream main bad21faf62, using the actual src/agents/mcp-oauth.ts provider and a local HTTP token endpoint. The published @modelcontextprotocol/sdk 1.29.0 auth/transport source was inspected before implementation.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran a pnpm exec tsx smoke that created a temp HOME/OpenClaw state dir, started a local HTTP token endpoint, saved old OAuth tokens through createMcpOAuthClientProvider, then issued two concurrent refresh_token POSTs through provider.wrapFetchForTokenRefresh(fetch).
Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "tokenEndpointCalls": 1,
  "first": {
    "access_token": "new-access",
    "refresh_token": "new-refresh",
    "token_type": "Bearer"
  },
  "second": {
    "refresh_token": "new-refresh",
    "access_token": "new-access",
    "token_type": "Bearer"
  },
  "stored": {
    "refresh_token": "new-refresh",
    "access_token": "new-access",
    "token_type": "Bearer"
  }
}
```
Observed result after fix: the local token endpoint received one request, both concurrent callers received the rotated refresh token, and the OpenClaw credential store ended with the rotated token.
What was not tested: live third-party OAuth provider with real rotating refresh tokens.

target.title: feat: add user-specific memory isolation
target.body:
## Summary

Adds user-specific memory isolation to OpenClaw, enabling per-user memory files in multi-user environments.

## Changes

### Core Feature
- **MemoryIsolationConfig** type added to `src/config/types.memory.ts`
  - `isolation.enabled` (default: `true`) to enable/disable isolation

### Session Key Extraction
- **extractUserIdFromSessionKey()** added to `src/sessions/session-key-utils.ts`
  - Extracts userId from direct message session keys
  - Returns `null` for channel/group sessions

### Memory Path Access Control
- **isMemoryPath()** updated in `src/memory/internal.ts`
  - When `userId` is provided, enforces access control
  - Users can only access their own `memory/{userId}/` directory
  - Shared memory files at `memory/` root are accessible to all

### Memory Tools
- **memory_search** and **memory_get** tools updated
  - Accept optional `senderId` parameter
  - Falls back to `senderId` from inbound context when session key doesn't contain userId

### Plugin Integration
- **extensions/memory-core/index.ts** updated
  - Passes `requesterSenderId` from plugin context to memory tools

## How It Works

```
Session Type       | userId Source          | Memory Path
-------------------|------------------------|---------------------------
Direct Message     | sessionKey extraction  | memory/{userId}/YYYY-MM-DD.md
Channel/Group     | requesterSenderId      | memory/{senderId}/YYYY-MM-DD.md
Disabled (legacy)  | none                   | memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md (shared)
```

## Security

- Users cannot access other users' memory directories
- Path validation in `isMemoryPath()` prevents directory traversal
- Shared memory at `memory/` root is accessible to all (for global context)

## Testing

- Unit tests added in `src/memory/internal.test.ts`
- All existing tests pass

## Breaking Changes

None. The feature is opt-in via configuration and defaults to the new isolated behavior when `memory.isolation.enabled` is not set (defaults to `true`).

Users who want the old shared behavior can set:
```json
{
  "memory": {
    "isolation": {
      "enabled": false
    }
  }
}
```

target.title: fix(usage): show provider quota in usage footer
target.body:
﻿Fixes #7379

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users who enable `/usage tokens` or `/usage full` would only see per-response token/cost details and still need to run `/status` separately to see active-provider quota windows such as GitHub Copilot Premium/Chat usage.

## Why This Change Was Made

This change reuses the existing provider-usage summary and formatter seams to append a compact active-provider quota summary to the reply usage footer when available. Provider fetching stays plugin-owned, the whole footer usage lookup is hard-timeout-bounded and omit-on-failure, and cost/session footer behavior remains limited to the existing `/usage full` path.

## User Impact

Users can now see provider quota windows directly in each `/usage tokens` or `/usage full` reply footer when their active provider exposes them. If credentials are missing, the usage endpoint is unavailable, or the quota lookup never resolves, replies still show the normal token/cost footer without failing or stalling indefinitely.

## Evidence

Claim proved:
- `/usage full` and `/usage tokens` reply footers append the active provider quota summary when provider usage data is available.
- Provider quota failure and a never-resolving provider usage lookup preserve the normal reply usage footer and omit quota text.
- The whole reply-footer provider usage lookup is bounded before final reply delivery.

Commands / artifacts:
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts --reporter=default`: passed, 51 tests.

- Local isolated CLI attempt: `OPENCLAW_HOME=<temp> OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<temp> OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --local --agent main --message "Reply exactly: USAGE_FOOTER_PROOF" --json --timeout 20` reached the real local agent path but returned provider timeout in this environment.

Result:

target.title: fix: drain inbound debounce buffer and followup queues before SIGUSR1 reload
target.body:
## Problem

When `config.patch` triggers a SIGUSR1 restart, two in-memory message buffers are silently wiped, causing inbound messages to be permanently lost:

1. **Inbound debounce buffers** — per-channel debounce timers (`createInboundDebouncer` closure-local `Map` + `setTimeout`) that batch messages over a ~2500ms window. On restart, the `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and buffered messages are garbage collected.

2. **Followup queues** — the `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` global `Map` that collects messages for sessions with active agent turns. Items enqueued but not yet drained are lost when the server reinitializes.

### Reproduction
Send a message on Signal → within 2.5s, trigger a config change (e.g. `config.patch`) → the message is silently dropped.

## Fix

### 1. Global debouncer registry (`src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts`)
- Each `createInboundDebouncer()` now auto-registers in a shared `Symbol.for('openclaw.inboundDebouncers')` global map
- New `flushAll()` method on each debouncer instance that immediately processes all buffered items (clears timeouts, calls `onFlush` callbacks)
- New exported `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` that iterates the registry, flushes all debouncers, and deregisters them

### 2. Followup queue drain waiter (`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts`)
- New `waitForFollowupQueueDrain(timeoutMs)` that polls `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` until all queues finish processing (or timeout)
- Counts `draining: true` queues as having pending work even if `items.length` is momentarily 0

### 3. SIGUSR1 restart integration (`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts`)
Before `markGatewayDraining()`, the restart flow now:
1. `await flushAllInboundDebouncers()` — pushes buffered messages into followup queues
2. `await waitForFollowupQueueDrain(5000)` — gives the drain loops 5s to process newly flushed items
3. Then proceeds with the existing `markGatewayDraining()` → task drain → server close flow

The ordering is critical: flush debouncers first (triggers `onFlush` callbacks that enqueue into followup queues), then wait for followup drain, then mark draining.

## Tests

- **`src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` flushes multiple registered debouncers, returns count, deregisters after flush; `createInboundDebouncer.flushAll` flushes all buffered keys
- **`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts`**: immediate return when empty, waits for drain completion, returns not-drained on timeout, counts actively draining queues as pending
- **`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`**: SIGUSR1 restart calls flush before `markGatewayDraining`, skips followup wait when no debouncers had buffered messages, logs warning on followup drain timeout

All 118 tests pass across the 4 affected test files. No type errors introduced.

## Files Changed
- `src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts` — global registry + `flushAll` + `flushAllInboundDebouncers()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts` — new `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue.ts` — barrel export
- `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` — hook into restart flow
- Test files for all of the above

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a SIGUSR1 restart fires within the inbound debounce window (~2.5s) or while a followup queue is mid-drain, buffered messages are silently dropped because the in-process `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and the closure-local `Map` is garbage collected. After this fix, `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` immediately flushes every registered debouncer into followup queues and `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()` blocks the SIGUSR1 path until those queues drain or a 5s ceiling is hit.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at HEAD `5d1e6592d02782361b871581c10a6b3c0329105f` of branch `fix/drain-queue-before-sigusr1`. Linux 6.12.67, Node v24.15.0, pnpm-driven monorepo. Patched modules loaded directly with `tsx` from the working tree (no rebuild step), and the built CLI (`pnpm openclaw --version`) reports `OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)` confirming the binary is built from the patched commit.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `pnpm openclaw --version` to confirm the live CLI is built from the patched HEAD.
  2. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303.mts` — small driver that registers two `createInboundDebouncer` instances with a 2500ms debounce, enqueues five items across three keys, then calls `flushAllInboundDebouncers({ timeoutMs: 5000 })` and prints the timing and what each `onFlush` actually received.
  3. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303-drain.mts` — driver that exercises `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` directly against the live `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` `Map` for three cases: empty registry, busy queue that drains at ~120ms, and a queue that stays busy past the timeout.
  4. `pnpm test src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts` — full targeted suites for all three changed files.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal capture from running the patched modules in this worktree.

  Live CLI build proves the binary contains the patched code:

  ```
  $ pnpm openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)
  ```

  `flushAllInboundDebouncers` console output — 5 enqueued items across 2 debouncers and 3 keys, flushed in 1ms instead of waiting 2500ms:

  ```
  [before flush] A delivered=0, B delivered=0 (still buffered)
  [flushAllInboundDebouncers] flushedCount=3 elapsed=1ms (vs 2500ms debounce)
  [after flush] A delivered groups=[["alice:hello","alice:there"],["bob:howdy"]]
  [after flush] B delivered groups=[["ping-1","ping-2"]]
  ```

  `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` console output — three live cases against the real `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` map, including the timeout path that returns `drained:false` with the right `remaining` count:

  ```
  [empty]         {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=0ms
  [busy->drained] {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=151ms
  [timeout]       {"drained":false,"remaining":2} elapsed=200ms
  ```

  Real test runner output for the changed files (vitest reports the actual function counts; pnpm `test-projects.mjs` shards by project):

  ```
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  24 passed (24)
     Duration  2.21s
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts
   Test Files  2 passed (2)
        Tests  71 passed (71)
     Duration  4.05s
  [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 11.96s
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` deterministically drains buffered Signal-style messages in 1 ms while the natural debounce window is 2500 ms, and reports the right buffer count (`flushedCount=3` for two keys in one debouncer plus one in another). `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` returns immediately when the registry is empty, observes the queue clearing in real time during the busy case (151 ms — one poll interval past the 120 ms cleanup), and correctly returns `drained:false` with `remaining=2` when the timeout fires before drain. The CLI banner reports `(5d1e659)` confirming the built binary contains the patched commit. With the SIGUSR1 hook in `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` calling these helpers in the right order before `markGatewayDraining()`, late inbound messages are pushed into followup queues and given up to 5 s to drain instead of being silently dropped.
- **What was not tested**: An end-to-end real-Signal SIGUSR1 reproduction was not executed on this machine (no signal-cli account paired here); the production restart path is exercised through `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`, but a live Signal → `config.patch` → restart roundtrip on real `signal-cli` was not run. Behavior under simultaneous restart-while-flushing on a hung `onFlush` (the registered handle is intentionally retained on flush failure for a future sweep) was not exercised live; that path is covered only by the unit tests in `src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`.

PR surface: Source +497, Tests +707, Docs +3. Total +1207 across 20 files.

target.title: fix(gateway): run daily session resets on schedule
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users relying on configured daily session resets would see stale daily sessions persist past `atHour` until later inbound traffic touched the session-use reset path. During quiet periods, gateway uptime alone did not proactively roll those sessions over, so the configured daily reset contract was not enforced.

## Why This Change Was Made

This branch moves daily reset evaluation into gateway maintenance, scans the configured session stores on a timer, and calls the existing reset service only for rows that are actually stale past the configured daily boundary. It intentionally preserves active runs and implicit provider-owned CLI sessions, and it reloads the current reset config on each sweep so policy changes take effect without a gateway restart.

## User Impact

Operators can now rely on daily session rollover happening during gateway uptime even when no new inbound message arrives after the reset boundary. Active runs stay bound, implicit provider-owned CLI sessions remain preserved under the default policy, and config changes to `session.reset`, `session.resetByType`, `session.resetByChannel`, or store targets are picked up on the next sweep.

## Evidence

Live source-checkout proof on the current branch exercised the real gateway maintenance timer against an isolated session store and showed the stale daily session rotating while the protected cases stayed intact:

```text
gateway-proof: before {"stale":"old-regular-session","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
[session-daily-reset] daily session reset sweep complete
gateway-proof: after {"stale":"5f959c12-f0ed-4ed1-99d2-cc158c66ac87","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
gateway-proof: staleReset true
gateway-proof: activePreserved true
gateway-proof: implicitCliPreserved true
```

Focused follow-up validation on the same head also passed:

```text
$ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       18 passed (18)

$ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
exit_code=0
```

## Linked context

- Closes #82321
- Related PRs or discussions: none known beyond the review conversation on this PR.
- Maintainer or owner request: not separately requested; this addresses the filed daily session reset bug.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Daily sessions stale past `atHour` are reset by gateway maintenance without waiting for a new inbound session use, while active runs and implicit provider-owned CLI sessions are preserved.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout in a Linux container, on branch `fix-daily-session-reset`, running the patched gateway maintenance timer against an isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`. Bundled plugin facades were disabled for the proof process with `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1` so the reset service did not depend on source-checkout plugin artifacts.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Ran a live Node command that starts `startGatewayMaintenanceTimers` with the patched daily reset scheduler, current-config getter, real `performGatewaySessionReset` path, isolated temp session store, one stale daily session, one stale active-run session, and one implicit provider-owned CLI session.
  - Command shape: `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... startGatewayMaintenanceTimers(...) ... TS`
  - Supplemental verification: `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts`
  - Supplemental type gates: `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  ```text
  $ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # creates isolated agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
  # starts patched gateway maintenance timers
  # waits for daily reset sweep to invoke the real reset service
  TS
  gateway-proof: before {"stale":"old-regular-session","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
  [session-daily-reset] daily session reset sweep complete
  gateway-proof: after {"stale":"5f959c12-f0ed-4ed1-99d2-cc158c66ac87","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
  gateway-proof: staleReset true
  gateway-proof: activePreserved true
  gateway-proof: implicitCliPreserved true

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests       18 passed (18)

  $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
  exit_code=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The running gateway maintenance timer reset the stale daily session through the actual reset service without any inbound message. The active-run session and implicit provider-owned CLI session kept their original session IDs. Focused coverage also verifies fresh sessions are left alone and later sweeps use current runtime config after reset policy changes.
- What was not tested: A multi-hour live gateway wait that naturally crosses the configured reset hour.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: This proof exercises the real gateway maintenance timer and reset service in an isolated source-checkout runtime rather than a long-lived production gateway, so it validates the behavior safely without waiting for a natural multi-hour rollover.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Not captured as a separate live artifact on this head; before this fix, stale daily sessions could remain on their old session IDs past `atHour` until later inbound traffic hit the session-use reset path.

## Tests and validation

- Commands run:
  - `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=<workspace>/.corepack node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-hooks.test.ts src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts`
  - `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=<workspace>/.corepack pnpm -s check:test-types`
  - `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=<workspace>/.corepack pnpm test:channels`
  - `git diff --check` after resolving the current `upstream/main` merge on the last branch refresh
  - `git merge --no-edit upstream/main` from `2e6e17f7c50237bb0dd8a40414d29d27ee80a9d8`, then pushed refreshed head `f5aa20fd4aab098a9483fb3f89a09b9e672deb70`
- Regression coverage added or updated: `src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts`, and `src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-hooks.test.ts`.
- What failed before this fix, if known? Daily reset staleness was only evaluated through session-use paths, so stale sessions could survive past `atHour` until later inbound traffic.
- If no test was added, why not? Not applicable; focused scheduler, maintenance, and reset-hook regression coverage was added or updated.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. Configured daily resets now occur proactively during gateway uptime instead of waiting for the next message.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.
- What is the highest-risk area? Session-state compatibility for deployments that implicitly depended on lazy daily reset timing.
- How is that risk mitigated? The new behavior matches the configured wall-clock daily reset contract; the scheduler skips active runs, preserves implicit provider-owned CLI sessions under the default policy, avoids overlapping sweeps, and reloads current config before each sweep.

## Current review state

- What is the next action? Await fresh current-head CI and ClawSweeper re-review on head `f5aa20fd4aab098a9483fb3f89a09b9e672deb70`.
- What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Fresh CI runs are needed after the clean `upstream/main` refresh that replaces the failed `QA Smoke CI` run from prior head `5c12af7bfdd3abed5b2cb3a8ab925fcccdfbc395`; no additional proof gap is open beyond the existing source-checkout real behavior proof already on this PR.
- Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? This refresh resolves the current worker lane by merging `upstream/main` `2e6e17f7c50237bb0dd8a40414d29d27ee80a9d8` onto the branch, preserving the daily session reset fix while replacing the stale failed current-head CI run with a fresh head for re-review.
